title: React.js
description: A staff-level tour of modern React (18/19) covering the rendering model, hooks semantics and pitfalls, reconciliation, state management trade-offs, React Server Components with the Next.js App Router, and concurrent-feature performance work. Written for engineers who must reason about correctness and cost, not just make things render.

blocks.text: React is a library for describing UI as a pure function of state. You do not imperatively mutate the DOM; you return a description (elements) of what the UI should look like for the current props and state, and React reconciles that description against the previous one to compute the minimal set of DOM mutations. Everything else in React, hooks, concurrency, Server Components, is machinery to make that model efficient, composable, and correct under real-world constraints.

At senior level the interesting questions are rarely 'how do I use useState'. They are: why does this component re-render, why is this closure stale, what runs on the server versus the client, and what is the actual cost of the abstraction I just reached for. This topic is organized around those questions.

Mental model to hold throughout: render is not commit. Rendering computes the element tree (may run multiple times, may be thrown away). Commit applies DOM changes and runs effects. Concurrent React can pause, resume, and discard renders, so render functions must be pure.

items: UI = f(state): components are functions from props/state to element trees.
React 18 introduced concurrent rendering (interruptible, prioritized renders).
React 19 stabilized Actions, use(), useOptimistic, and shipped alongside the React Compiler.
Server Components move component execution to the server, changing where code and data live.

blocks.text: Before React, UI code was a maze of imperative mutations: find a node, toggle a class, append a child, keep it consistent with three other pieces of state. The bug class was 'the DOM and the data drifted apart'. React's insight is to make the DOM a derived value. You never update the DOM; you update state and re-derive. Consistency becomes structural rather than something you defend by hand.

The Virtual DOM is the enabling trick: keep a lightweight JS representation of the tree, diff the new tree against the old, and apply only the differences. The VDOM is not fast in absolute terms; a hand-tuned imperative update is faster. It is fast enough while being dramatically simpler to reason about, and it unlocks features that raw DOM code cannot easily offer: interruptible rendering, offscreen prerendering, and the same component model on server and client.

Interview trap: 'the Virtual DOM makes React fast' is imprecise. The VDOM makes React predictable and composable; performance comes from batching, bailout heuristics, and letting you avoid unnecessary work. Say that.

items: Declarative code is testable: given state, the output is deterministic.
Diffing lets React own scheduling, enabling concurrent features.
The same programming model spans DOM, native (React Native), and server rendering.

sections.heading: JSX, Elements, and the Render/Commit Split

blocks.text: JSX is syntax sugar. <Button onClick={fn}>Save</Button> compiles (via the automatic runtime) to jsx(Button, { onClick: fn, children: 'Save' }). The result is a plain object, a React element, describing what to render. It is not a DOM node and does nothing until React renders it. This is why elements are cheap to create and why you can return them, store them, and pass them around freely.

Two phases matter. Render phase: React calls your component functions to produce the element tree and diffs it. This phase must be pure, no side effects, no DOM reads, because concurrent React may run it multiple times or abandon it. Commit phase: React applies the computed mutations to the real DOM and then runs layout effects (synchronously) and passive effects (after paint). Distinguishing these is the key to explaining useEffect vs useLayoutEffect and why side effects belong in effects, not in the render body.

Never mutate props, refs, or module-level state during render, and never trigger network calls there. In StrictMode React intentionally double-invokes render and effect setup in development to surface impure code. Bugs that only appear in prod builds are often impurity StrictMode already tried to warn you about.

sections.heading: Hooks and the Rules of Hooks

blocks.text: Hooks are how function components get memory and lifecycle. The Rules of Hooks are not stylistic: call hooks only at the top level of a component or another hook, never inside conditions, loops, or after an early return. The reason is mechanical. React stores hook state in a per-component linked list and matches each hook call to its slot by call order, not by name. If the order changes between renders, useState number two on this render lines up with useEffect number two from last render, and everything corrupts.

items: Top-level only: no hooks in if/for/&&/ternary/try or after return.
Call from React functions only: components or custom hooks, not plain utilities.
Order must be stable across every render of a given component instance.
The eslint-plugin-react-hooks rules (rules-of-hooks + exhaustive-deps) catch most violations at lint time.

blocks.text: Because identity is positional, you can safely call the same hook many times (useState twice) but never conditionally. This constraint is exactly what lets hooks be simple functions instead of requiring a registration ceremony.

useState returns the current value and a setter. State is per-instance and persists across renders. Two subtleties dominate interviews: batching and the functional updater. In React 18, all state updates are batched by default, including those inside promises, setTimeout, and native event handlers (this was not true in React 17, where only React event handlers batched). Multiple setState calls in the same tick produce a single re-render.

Use the functional form whenever the next state depends on the previous state, or when the setter is captured in a closure (an event listener, an effect, a debounce) where count could be stale. Prefer it by default in shared hooks; it removes count from your effect dependency arrays and eliminates a whole class of stale-value bugs.

State updates are asynchronous with respect to the surrounding code. Reading count immediately after setCount(count + 1) gives the old value; the new value is only visible on the next render. If you truly need to opt out of batching (rare, e.g. measuring the DOM between two updates), flushSync exists but forces a synchronous re-render and hurts performance.

sections.heading: useEffect: Dependencies, Cleanup, and When Not To

blocks.text: useEffect synchronizes a component with an external system: subscriptions, timers, network requests, non-React widgets, the document title. The dependency array declares what the effect reads from render scope; React re-runs the effect (after running the previous cleanup) whenever a dependency changes by Object.is. An empty array means 'set up once, clean up on unmount'. Omitting the array runs it after every render. Lying about dependencies to silence the linter reintroduces stale closures.

The most senior thing you can say about effects is when NOT to use one. You do not need an effect to transform data for rendering (compute it during render, memoize if expensive), to reset state on prop change (use a key), to handle a user event (put the logic in the handler), or to derive state from props (derive it, do not mirror it into state). Effects are for synchronizing with systems outside React. Most 'effect bugs' are effects that should not have existed.

items: Compute during render, do not stuff derived data into state via an effect.
Reset component state by giving it a new key, not an effect that watches props.
Event-specific logic belongs in the event handler, not in an effect that reacts to state.
If an effect only exists to notify a parent, lift the call into the handler instead.

blocks.text: useEffect runs after paint (passive); useLayoutEffect runs synchronously after DOM mutation but before paint. Use useLayoutEffect only to read layout (measure a node) and mutate the DOM before the browser paints, e.g. positioning a tooltip, to avoid a visible flicker. It blocks paint, so overuse hurts perceived performance.

A custom hook is just a function that calls hooks and follows the Rules of Hooks. It packages stateful logic for reuse. Crucially, two components using the same hook do NOT share state; each call site gets its own state. Custom hooks are the primary unit of logic reuse in modern React, replacing HOCs and render props for most cases.

Good custom hooks have a narrow, honest name (useDebounce, useOnScreen, useLocalStorage), return a stable API, and keep effects internal. If a hook returns callbacks, memoize them with useCallback so consumers can safely depend on them.

useRef gives you a mutable container (ref.current) that persists across renders but whose mutation does NOT trigger a re-render. Two uses: holding a DOM node (ref on a JSX element), and storing mutable values that should survive renders without causing them, timer ids, previous values, the latest callback, whether a component is mounted.

items: Use state when the value should be reflected in the UI and drive re-renders.
Use a ref when the value is instrumental, not visual (ids, latest-value caches, DOM handles).
Never read/write ref.current during render to compute output; it breaks purity and concurrency.

blocks.text: Common mistake: using a ref to avoid a re-render for a value the UI actually depends on. The screen then shows stale data. Refs are for values the render output does not read.

sections.heading: useMemo, useCallback, React.memo, and the Compiler

blocks.text: These three tools reduce work, but each has a cost (memory + comparison + code noise) and none is free. useMemo caches a computed value. useCallback caches a function identity. React.memo skips re-rendering a component when its props are shallow-equal to the previous render. They only help when the thing you are memoizing is genuinely expensive or when a stable identity is needed to make a downstream React.memo or effect dependency work.

rows: Cheap computations; deps change every render
Give a stable prop to a memo'd child or a stable effect dep
Child is not memoized; function recreated deps churn
Skip re-render when props are shallow-equal
Props include new object/array/function each render

blocks.text: React 19's React Compiler auto-memoizes at build time by analyzing your code, making most manual useMemo/useCallback unnecessary. In a compiler-enabled codebase, write plain idiomatic code; only reach for manual memoization where the compiler bails out (it tells you). Do not pre-optimize by memoizing everything by hand.

When NOT to memoize: cheap computations, values that change every render anyway (memo overhead with no bailout), and components that are already fast. Premature memoization adds indirection, hides bugs (stale deps), and can even slow things down. Measure with the Profiler first; memoize the hot path, not everything.

Reconciliation is React's diffing algorithm. It compares the new element tree to the previous one. Rules: different element types (div vs span, ComponentA vs ComponentB) tear down the old subtree and rebuild; same type reuses the DOM node and updates props; lists use keys to match elements across renders. Keys tell React which item is which so it can move, keep, or remove DOM nodes and preserve component state and DOM focus.

Why index keys break: with key={i}, inserting or reordering makes React think item at index 0 simply changed props, rather than a new item being inserted before it. It reuses the wrong DOM nodes and component instances. The visible symptoms are: input values attached to the wrong row, checkbox state jumping to another item, and lost focus. For static lists that never reorder, index keys are acceptable, but a stable id is always safer.

Changing a component's key intentionally resets it: <Form key={userId} /> throws away and recreates the form when userId changes, clearing all internal state. This is the idiomatic way to 'reset state when a prop changes', far better than an effect.

A controlled input has its value driven by React state (value + onChange); React is the single source of truth. An uncontrolled input holds its own state in the DOM, and you read it via a ref (or on submit) with defaultValue setting the initial value. Controlled inputs give you validation-on-keystroke, formatting, and derived UI, at the cost of a render per keystroke. Uncontrolled inputs are lighter and closer to plain HTML, useful for large forms and file inputs (which are always uncontrolled).

Do not switch a single input from uncontrolled to controlled mid-life (value going from undefined to a string). React warns and behavior is confusing. Pick one; if controlled, initialize state to '' not undefined.

sections.heading: Context API and Its Re-render Caveat

blocks.text: Context passes a value down the tree without prop drilling. Every component that calls useContext(MyContext) re-renders whenever the Provider's value changes by Object.is, regardless of whether it uses the part that changed. This is the central caveat: Context is a broadcast, not a fine-grained subscription. Putting frequently changing state (like a live cursor position) in a wide-scoped context re-renders the whole subtree.

items: Split contexts by change frequency: a stable 'dispatch' context + a volatile 'state' context.
Memoize the Provider value object to avoid gratuitous re-renders.
For high-frequency or selector-based needs, reach for Zustand/Jotai or use(Context) with a selector library.
Context is for dependency injection (theme, auth, locale), not a general state manager.

blocks.text: Context does not have built-in selectors: you cannot subscribe to just one field. That is precisely why external stores (Zustand, Redux, Jotai) exist, they offer selector-based subscriptions so a component only re-renders when its slice changes.

An error boundary is a component that catches render-phase errors in its subtree and shows a fallback instead of crashing the whole app. As of React 19 there is still no hook form; boundaries must be class components implementing getDerivedStateFromError and/or componentDidCatch, or you use a library like react-error-boundary. They do NOT catch errors in event handlers, async code, or the server (handle those with try/catch).

Place boundaries strategically: one at the app root for last-resort UI, plus finer boundaries around independent widgets (a chart, a comment thread) so one failing panel does not blank the page. Error boundaries pair naturally with Suspense fallbacks.

sections.heading: State Management: Client vs Server State

blocks.text: The most important distinction is client state vs server state. Server state (data fetched from an API) is asynchronous, shared, cached, and can go stale; it wants caching, revalidation, deduping, and background refetch, exactly what React Query (TanStack Query) or RTK Query provide. Client state (UI toggles, wizard steps, selected tab) is synchronous and local; Zustand, Jotai, Redux Toolkit, or plain useState/useReducer fit. A frequent architectural mistake is dumping fetched data into Redux and hand-rolling caching that React Query gives for free.

rows: Large apps needing structure, devtools, middleware
Lightweight global client state, minimal ceremony
Fewer conventions; you design the structure
Fine-grained derived state, avoiding re-render sprawl
Atom graph can get hard to trace at scale
Fetching, caching, revalidation of async data
Not for client UI state; adds a cache to reason about

blocks.text: Rule of thumb: use React Query (or RTK Query) for anything that came from the network; use useState/useReducer for local component state; reach for a global client store (Zustand/Jotai/Redux) only when unrelated components must share synchronous UI state.

sections.heading: React Server Components and the Next.js App Router

blocks.text: Server Components (RSCs) render on the server, never ship their code to the client, and can access server-only resources directly (database, filesystem, secrets) with async/await. They cannot use state, effects, refs, or browser APIs, they have no client runtime. Client Components (marked with the 'use client' directive at the top of the file) are the interactive parts: hooks, event handlers, browser APIs. In the Next.js App Router, components are Server Components by default; you opt into the client at the leaves of your tree.

Server Actions ('use server') let client components call server functions directly as async functions, no manual API route. They power form submissions and mutations with progressive enhancement. Layouts wrap route segments and persist across navigations (their state is preserved), while pages are the leaf UI. Data flows server -> client as serializable props; you cannot pass functions or class instances from a Server to a Client Component (only Server Actions and plain data).

items: Default to Server Components; add 'use client' only where you need interactivity.
Push 'use client' down to the leaves to keep the JS bundle small.
Server Components can import and render Client Components, but not vice-versa (a client file can only render server content passed as children).
'use server' marks server actions callable from the client; validate inputs, they are a public endpoint.

blocks.text: You cannot pass non-serializable props (functions, Dates round-trip but class instances do not, Symbols) from a Server to a Client Component. Event handlers must originate inside the client component or be Server Actions. Getting this boundary wrong is the number one App Router error.

Suspense lets a component 'suspend' while data or code loads, showing a fallback until it is ready, and enables streaming SSR (the server sends the shell immediately and streams in slower parts). Concurrent features let React keep the UI responsive during heavy updates. useTransition marks a state update as non-urgent so React can interrupt it to handle urgent input; useDeferredValue defers re-rendering of a value derived from fast-changing state.

useTransition vs useDeferredValue: use useTransition when YOU own the state update and can wrap the setter (mark the update low priority). Use useDeferredValue when you receive a value you cannot control the update of (e.g. from props) and want to render a lagging copy. Both keep input responsive; they differ in where you can intervene.

Once correctness is solid, the big wins are: render fewer components, ship less JavaScript, and do heavy work off the critical path. Virtualization renders only the visible slice of a long list (react-window / TanStack Virtual) so 10,000 rows cost ~20 DOM nodes. Code splitting with React.lazy + Suspense (or Next.js dynamic imports) defers loading of routes and heavy components until needed, shrinking the initial bundle.

items: Virtualize any list beyond a few hundred rows; do not memo your way around a giant DOM.
Code-split at route boundaries and around heavy leaf components (editors, charts, maps).
Use the React Profiler to find the actual re-render hot spots before optimizing.
Prefer moving expensive work to the server (RSC) or a Web Worker over micro-optimizing renders.

blocks.text: Order of operations for a slow list: (1) fix accidental re-renders / stabilize props, (2) memoize the truly expensive row if needed, (3) virtualize. Virtualization is usually the highest-leverage change; memoization alone cannot fix a 10k-node DOM.

sections.heading: Production Example: A Data Table Widget

blocks.text: A realistic composition: server-state via React Query, a debounced search, an error boundary, and a virtualized body. This is the shape of a real feature and shows the tools working together rather than in isolation.
Note placeholderData keeps the previous page's rows visible during a refetch so the table does not flash a spinner on every keystroke, a small detail that separates polished apps from janky ones.

items: 17 -> 18: switch to createRoot; automatic batching now covers promises/timeouts (audit code that relied on separate renders); StrictMode double-invokes effects in dev, fix effects that are not idempotent.

18 -> 19: ReactDOM.render and legacy context are gone; forwardRef is largely unnecessary (ref is a regular prop for function components); adopt use(), Actions, and useOptimistic where they simplify code.
React Compiler: enable it and delete most manual useMemo/useCallback once verified; keep manual memo only where the compiler reports a bailout.
Adopting RSC: migrate incrementally by route in the App Router; move data fetching into async Server Components and reserve 'use client' for interactive leaves.
propTypes and defaultProps on function components are removed in 19; use TypeScript and default parameter values instead.

blocks.text: The most common 18 upgrade surprise is StrictMode running effect setup->cleanup->setup twice in development. If an effect breaks under that (double subscriptions, duplicate fetches), the effect was not correctly cleaning up, fix it rather than removing StrictMode.

diagrams.title: Render and Commit Pipeline
diagrams.content:
flowchart TD
  A[State or props change] --> B[Render phase: call components]
  B --> C[Produce new element tree]
  C --> D[Reconcile: diff vs previous tree]
  D --> E{Any changes?}
  E -- No --> F[Bail out, no DOM work]
  E -- Yes --> G[Commit phase: apply DOM mutations]
  G --> H[Run useLayoutEffect sync]
  H --> I[Browser paints]
  I --> J[Run useEffect passive]

diagrams.title: RSC Request Flow: Server to Client
diagrams.content:
flowchart TD
  A[Browser requests route] --> B[Next.js server runs Server Components]
  B --> C[Fetch data, access DB and secrets]
  C --> D[Serialize RSC payload plus client component refs]
  D --> E[Stream HTML shell then chunks]
  E --> F[Client receives payload]
  F --> G[Hydrate only Client Components]
  G --> H[Interactive UI, state and effects run]

diagrams.title: Why Hook Call Order Matters
diagrams.content:
Render N            Render N+1 (hook skipped by an if)
[0] useState count  [0] useEffect  <- MISMATCH
[1] useEffect       [1] useRef     <- corrupted
[2] useRef          ...
React matches hooks by index, not name -> order MUST be stable.

questions.question: Why can't you call hooks conditionally or in a loop?
questions.answer: React associates hook state with a component instance by call order, storing each hook's state in a positional linked list, not by any name or key. On every render it walks that list in the same sequence, matching the first useState call to slot 0, the second to slot 1, and so on. If a condition skips a hook on some renders, the indices shift and a later render's useState lines up with a previous render's useEffect slot, corrupting state. Keeping all hooks at the top level, unconditionally, guarantees the order is identical every render. You branch on values after the hooks run, not around the hook calls themselves. The eslint rules-of-hooks lint catches most violations.
followUps: What data structure backs hook state?
Why is this different from class component state?

questions.question: Why is using the array index as a key dangerous?
questions.answer: Keys identify which list item is which across renders so React can move, keep, or remove the right DOM nodes and component instances. With an index key, inserting or reordering shifts every index, so React thinks the item that is now at index 0 is the same one that was there before and merely changed props. It reuses the wrong DOM node and internal state. The visible symptoms are input values or checkbox states attached to the wrong row, and lost focus. For lists that never reorder or receive insertions, index keys are acceptable, but a stable id derived from the data is always safer. This is a very common source of subtle stateful-list bugs.
followUps: When are index keys acceptable?
How does changing a key reset a component?

questions.question: What is a stale closure in a useEffect and how do you fix it?
questions.answer: A stale closure happens when an effect (or a callback it registers, like an interval or event listener) captures variables from the render scope in which it was created and keeps using those old values because the effect did not re-run. For example, an interval set up in an empty-deps effect will forever see the initial count. Fixes: include the value in the dependency array so the effect re-subscribes with fresh values; use the functional updater setCount(c => c + 1) so you never read the captured value; or store the latest value/callback in a ref and read ref.current inside the callback. The exhaustive-deps lint flags the missing dependency that usually causes this. The wrong fix is to disable the lint and lie about dependencies.
followUps: Show the ref-latest-callback pattern.
Why does the functional updater avoid the problem?

questions.question: When should you NOT memoize with useMemo/useCallback?
questions.answer: Memoization has real costs: extra memory to hold the cached value, a dependency comparison every render, and code noise that can hide stale-value bugs. Do not memoize cheap computations, because the comparison can cost more than recomputing. Do not useCallback a function passed to a child that is not wrapped in React.memo, since the child re-renders anyway. Do not memoize when the dependencies change every render, because you pay the cost and never get a cache hit. In React 19 with the Compiler enabled, most manual memoization is unnecessary because the compiler inserts it automatically where beneficial. Profile first and memoize the measured hot path rather than reflexively wrapping everything.
followUps: What does the React Compiler change?
How do you find the real hot path?

questions.question: Why does Context cause re-renders, and how do you mitigate it?
questions.answer: Every component that calls useContext re-renders whenever the Provider's value changes by Object.is, regardless of whether it reads the part that changed, because Context is a broadcast without selectors. A frequent bug is passing a fresh object literal as the value each render, which changes identity every time and forces all consumers to re-render unconditionally. Mitigations: memoize the Provider value with useMemo; split one context into a stable context (dispatch/actions) and a volatile context (fast-changing state) so consumers subscribe to only what they need; and for fine-grained needs use an external store like Zustand or Jotai that supports selector-based subscriptions. Context is best for dependency injection such as theme, auth, or locale, not as a general-purpose state manager.
followUps: Why doesn't Context support selectors?
When would you split a context in two?

questions.question: Why can't a Server Component use state or effects?
questions.answer: Server Components execute once on the server to produce a serialized description of UI; their code never ships to the browser and there is no client runtime to hold state or schedule effects. State and effects are inherently interactive and client-side, they respond to user input and lifecycle over time, which only exists where React is running in the browser. So useState, useEffect, useRef, and browser APIs are unavailable in Server Components. Anything interactive must live in a Client Component marked with 'use client'. The benefit is that Server Components can be async, fetch data directly, access secrets, and add zero JavaScript to the bundle. You compose the two: Server Components render the static shell and pass serializable data down to Client Component islands.
followUps: What can you pass from a Server to a Client Component?
How do Server Actions fit in?

questions.question: useTransition vs useDeferredValue: when do you use each?
questions.answer: Both keep the UI responsive by deprioritizing non-urgent renders, but they intervene at different points. Use useTransition when you own the state update and can wrap the setter: startTransition(() => setState(...)) marks that update low priority so React can interrupt it to handle urgent input, and it gives you an isPending flag. Use useDeferredValue when you receive a value you cannot control the update of, such as a prop or a value set elsewhere; it returns a lagging copy that React updates at low priority while the original stays current for urgent uses. In short: useTransition wraps the update, useDeferredValue wraps the value. A typical search box uses one or the other to keep typing smooth while the expensive filtered list renders behind.
followUps: Which gives you an isPending flag?
Can you combine them?

questions.question: Why does calling setCount(count+1) three times only add one, and how does the functional updater fix it?
questions.answer: Within a single event, count is a constant captured from the current render; each setCount(count + 1) computes the same old value plus one, and because React 18 batches the updates into one re-render, the last write wins and you get plus one, not plus three. The functional updater setCount(c => c + 1) instead receives the latest queued state as its argument, so three calls chain to plus three regardless of batching. Use the functional form whenever the next state depends on the previous state or when the setter is captured in a closure that might be stale, such as inside a timer or effect. It also lets you drop the state value from effect dependency arrays. Batching itself is a performance feature: multiple updates in one tick cause a single render.
followUps: When did automatic batching expand in React 18?
How does flushSync relate?

questions.question: Why do effects need cleanup functions, and what does aborting a fetch accomplish?
questions.answer: An effect's cleanup runs before the effect re-runs and on unmount, undoing whatever the effect set up: clearing timers, unsubscribing, or cancelling requests. Without cleanup you leak subscriptions and can update state on an unmounted component. For fetches, passing an AbortController signal and calling controller.abort() in cleanup cancels an in-flight request when the dependency changes or the component unmounts. This prevents race conditions where a slow earlier request resolves after a newer one and overwrites correct data with stale data, and it avoids state updates after unmount. In StrictMode's development double-invoke, correct cleanup is what makes the setup/cleanup/setup cycle harmless. Effects that lack cleanup are the usual cause of duplicate subscriptions and out-of-order data.
followUps: How does this interact with StrictMode?
What is the race condition it prevents?

questions.question: What is the difference between useEffect and useLayoutEffect?
questions.answer: useEffect is a passive effect: it runs after the browser has painted, so it does not block visual updates and is the right default for data fetching, subscriptions, and logging. useLayoutEffect runs synchronously after React mutates the DOM but before the browser paints, so it can read layout (measure a node) and make DOM changes the user will see in the same frame, avoiding a visible flicker. Use it for things like measuring an element to position a tooltip or synchronizing scroll position. Because it blocks paint, overusing useLayoutEffect hurts perceived performance. On the server useLayoutEffect does not run and warns, so guard SSR code accordingly. Rule of thumb: default to useEffect and reach for useLayoutEffect only when you must read or write layout before paint.
followUps: Why does useLayoutEffect warn during SSR?
Give a concrete flicker example.

questions.question: Name situations where you should NOT use an effect.
questions.answer: Effects synchronize with external systems; a lot of code that reaches for them should not. You do not need an effect to transform data for rendering, compute it during render and memoize if it is expensive. You do not need one to reset state when a prop changes, give the component a new key instead. You do not need one to handle a user event, put that logic in the event handler where the event happened. You do not need one to derive state from props, derive it inline rather than mirroring props into state that can drift. And you rarely need one just to notify a parent, call the parent's callback from the handler. Removing unnecessary effects eliminates whole classes of stale-closure and double-run bugs; react.dev's 'You Might Not Need an Effect' catalogs these.
followUps: How do you reset state without an effect?
Why is mirroring props into state a bug?

questions.question: When do you use a ref instead of state?
questions.answer: Use state for values the UI renders and that should trigger a re-render when they change. Use a ref for values that must persist across renders but whose changes should not cause a re-render, such as a timer id, the previous value of a prop, a mutable latest-callback cache, or a handle to a DOM node. Mutating ref.current is synchronous and does not schedule a render, which is exactly why it suits instrumental values the render output does not read. The common mistake is putting UI-relevant data in a ref to avoid re-renders, which then shows stale content because nothing tells React to re-render. So the test is simple: if the screen depends on the value, use state; if it does not, a ref is appropriate.
followUps: Why not read a ref during render?
How do you get a DOM node with a ref?

questions.question: How does React's reconciliation algorithm decide what to update?
questions.answer: Reconciliation diffs the newly rendered element tree against the previous one. If an element's type differs from before (a div replaced by a span, or ComponentA by ComponentB), React tears down the old subtree, including its state, and mounts a fresh one. If the type is the same, React keeps the existing DOM node and instance and updates only the changed props. For lists of children it uses keys to match elements across renders so it can reorder, insert, or remove nodes while preserving the right state and DOM identity. This heuristic is O(n) instead of the O(n cubed) of a general tree diff, trading theoretical optimality for speed. Understanding it explains why changing a key resets a component and why moving a component between parents can remount it.
followUps: Why is it O(n) not O(n^3)?
What happens when a component moves parents?

questions.question: Explain controlled vs uncontrolled components and when to use each.
questions.answer: A controlled input has its value bound to React state via value and onChange, making React the single source of truth; this enables instant validation, formatting, and derived UI at the cost of a re-render per keystroke. An uncontrolled input keeps its value in the DOM, you set an initial defaultValue and read the current value via a ref, typically on submit; it is lighter and closer to plain HTML. Use controlled for inputs whose value drives other UI or needs live validation, and uncontrolled for simple or very large forms where per-keystroke renders are wasteful. File inputs are always uncontrolled. A classic warning arises if you switch a single input from uncontrolled to controlled mid-life by letting value go from undefined to a string, so pick one and initialize controlled state to an empty string.
followUps: Why are file inputs always uncontrolled?
Why is the undefined-to-value switch a problem?

questions.question: What do error boundaries catch and what do they miss?
questions.answer: Error boundaries catch errors thrown during rendering, in lifecycle methods, and in constructors of the components below them, and they render a fallback UI instead of unmounting the whole tree. They are implemented as class components with getDerivedStateFromError (to render fallback) and componentDidCatch (to log), since there is still no hook equivalent in React 19; libraries like react-error-boundary wrap this. They do NOT catch errors in event handlers, asynchronous code such as setTimeout or promises, server-side rendering, or errors thrown in the boundary itself, handle those with ordinary try/catch. Best practice is a root boundary for last-resort UI plus finer boundaries around independent widgets so one failure does not blank the page, and they pair naturally with Suspense fallbacks.
followUps: How do you catch async/event errors?
Where should boundaries be placed?

questions.question: Why distinguish server state from client state, and which tools fit each?
questions.answer: Server state is data owned by a backend: it is asynchronous, shared across clients, cached, and can become stale, so it needs deduping, caching, background revalidation, and request cancellation. React Query (TanStack Query) or RTK Query provide exactly that, so you should not hand-roll it in a Redux store. Client state is synchronous and local, UI toggles, selected tabs, wizard steps, and fits useState/useReducer, or a small global store like Zustand or Jotai when unrelated components must share it. A common architectural mistake is dumping fetched data into Redux and reinventing caching, staleness, and refetching. The rule of thumb: network data goes in a server-state cache, local UI state goes in component or a lightweight global store, and Redux Toolkit is warranted mainly when a large app benefits from its structure, devtools, and middleware.
followUps: When is Redux Toolkit still the right call?
How does Jotai differ from Zustand?

questions.question: Is the Virtual DOM what makes React fast?
questions.answer: Not exactly, and this is a common misconception. A hand-tuned imperative DOM update is faster in raw terms than diffing a Virtual DOM; the VDOM adds overhead. What the VDOM buys is predictability and composability: you describe UI as a function of state and React figures out the minimal mutations, which makes code far easier to reason about and enables features like concurrent rendering, offscreen prerendering, and the same model on server and client. React's actual performance comes from batching updates, bailing out of unnecessary re-renders, and letting you avoid work. So the honest answer is that the VDOM makes React fast enough while being dramatically simpler, and genuine performance work is about rendering fewer components and shipping less JavaScript, not the diff itself.
followUps: What actually drives React performance?
What does the VDOM enable that raw DOM cannot?

questions.question: What can and cannot cross the Server/Client Component boundary?
questions.answer: Data flows from Server Components to Client Components as serializable props: strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, plain objects, and Server Actions (functions marked 'use server'). You cannot pass arbitrary functions, class instances, Symbols, or other non-serializable values, event handlers must originate inside the client component or be Server Actions. A Server Component can import and render a Client Component, but a Client Component cannot import a Server Component; it can only render server content that is passed to it as children (the children slot pattern). The 'use client' directive marks the boundary at the top of a file, and everything imported by that file becomes part of the client bundle. Getting this boundary wrong, especially passing a function prop from server to client, is the most common App Router error.
followUps: What is the children slot pattern?
How do Server Actions replace API routes?

challenges.title: Build a useFetch hook with loading, error, and abort
challenges.prompt: Implement a generic useFetch<T>(url) hook that returns { data, error, loading }. It must refetch when the url changes, cancel any in-flight request on url change or unmount using AbortController, ignore abort errors, and never set state after the effect has been cleaned up. Keep it type-safe.
hints: Use an AbortController inside the effect and abort it in the cleanup function.
Distinguish AbortError (ignore it) from real errors before calling setError.
Reset loading to true and clear previous data/error at the start of each fetch.
challenges.explanation: The hook stores all three fields in a single state object so updates are atomic and consistent. On each render where url changes, the effect creates a fresh AbortController and immediately resets to a loading state, clearing stale data so the UI does not show the previous url's result. The fetch checks res.ok because fetch only rejects on network failure, not on HTTP error statuses, so a 404 must be turned into an error manually. In the catch we ignore AbortError, because aborting is our own intentional cancellation rather than a real failure, and only genuine errors update state. The cleanup returns controller.abort(), which cancels an in-flight request when url changes or the component unmounts; this prevents both race conditions where a slower earlier request overwrites a newer result and warnings from setting state after unmount, since an aborted fetch rejects with AbortError that we swallow. The dependency array is exactly [url], so the effect re-runs precisely when the input changes. In production you would layer React Query on top for caching and dedupe, but this shows the underlying mechanics.

challenges.title: Build a debounced search box
challenges.prompt: Create a SearchBox component that lets the user type freely (input stays instant) but only issues a search request after 400ms of no typing. Cancel outdated requests, show a loading indicator during the fetch, and avoid searching on an empty query.
hints: Keep the input controlled by fast local state; derive a debounced value from it.
Debounce with a setTimeout cleared in the effect cleanup on every keystroke.
Fetch in an effect keyed on the debounced value, with an AbortController for cancellation.
challenges.explanation: The input is controlled by query, updated on every keystroke, so typing feels instant with no lag. The useDebounce hook holds a separate debounced value that only catches up 400ms after the last change, because each keystroke resets the setTimeout via the cleanup clearing the previous timer. The search effect depends solely on debounced, so it fires only when the settled value changes, not on every character. It guards against empty or whitespace-only queries by clearing results and returning early, which avoids pointless requests. An AbortController cancels any in-flight request when a newer debounced value arrives, preventing a race where a slow earlier search resolves last and shows stale results; AbortError is ignored because it is our own cancellation. The loading flag gives the user feedback during the request and is cleared on both success and real error. This pattern generalizes to any type-ahead UI and is exactly what React Query's queryKey plus debounced input would express more declaratively.

challenges.title: Fix an infinite-loop useEffect
hints: options is an object literal recreated with a new identity on every render.
Object dependencies are compared by reference (Object.is), not by contents.
Either move the constant out, memoize it, or depend on primitive fields.
challenges.explanation: The loop happens because options is a fresh object literal created on every render, and React compares dependencies with Object.is, which treats two structurally identical objects as different when their references differ. So the effect runs, calls setUser, which triggers a re-render, which creates a new options object, which the effect sees as a changed dependency, and it runs again forever. The core lesson is that object, array, and function dependencies are compared by reference, not by value. Option A recognizes that the options are actually constant and hoists them to module scope, giving a stable identity so they can be dropped from the dependency array entirely and the effect keys only on userId. Option B handles the genuinely-dynamic case by wrapping the object in useMemo keyed on its primitive fields, so its identity only changes when include actually changes. Both versions also add an active flag in cleanup to ignore a resolved fetch after the effect re-runs or the component unmounts, preventing out-of-order updates. In a compiler-enabled React 19 codebase the memoization would often be inserted automatically, but understanding the reference-equality cause is what lets you diagnose these loops.

challenges.title: Optimize a slow list with memo and virtualization
challenges.prompt: A list of 10,000 rows re-renders every row on any state change and scrolls sluggishly. Improve it so that only visible rows are in the DOM and unaffected rows do not re-render. Assume each Row is moderately expensive.
hints: Stabilize the row's callback props with useCallback so React.memo can bail out.
Wrap Row in React.memo to skip re-rendering rows whose props are unchanged.
Virtualize the list so only the visible window of rows is mounted.
challenges.explanation: The original list is slow for two independent reasons, and the fix addresses both. First, all 10,000 rows are mounted in the DOM at once, which is expensive to lay out and scroll regardless of React; virtualization solves this by rendering only the visible window plus a small overscan, so the DOM holds roughly twenty nodes instead of ten thousand while a spacer div preserves the correct scroll height. Second, on any state change every Row re-renders because it is not memoized and, even if it were, an inline onSelect would be a new function each render and defeat the bailout. Wrapping Row in React.memo makes it skip re-rendering when its props are shallow-equal, and useCallback gives onSelect a stable identity so changing selected does not invalidate every row's props. The two techniques are complementary: memoization stops needless re-renders of the rows that are mounted, and virtualization stops most rows from being mounted at all. Virtualization is usually the higher-leverage change because memoization alone cannot fix a ten-thousand-node DOM. In a React Compiler codebase the memo and useCallback are often inserted automatically, but the virtualization is still required.

references.label: You Might Not Need an Effect
Overreacted: A Complete Guide to useEffect
